title: Pigs in blankets
subtitle: Chipolatas wrapped in streaky bacon, roasted until crisp
excerpt: Small pork sausages wrapped tightly in streaky bacon and roasted until the bacon is crisp and the sausages are browned. The most anticipated component of the Christmas dinner.
sourceNotes: Standard British Christmas accompaniment. Pigs in blankets became a British Christmas dinner staple from the mid-twentieth century.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze unbaked on a tray, then transfer to a bag. Cook from frozen at 200 degrees C fan for 30 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Makes large batches easily. Use extra baking trays with space between each piece.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Wrap the sausages up to 24 hours ahead and refrigerate. Bring to room temperature 30 minutes before roasting.

glossaryTerms.definition: The process of fat melting out of the bacon during roasting, which bastes the sausage and produces the crisp, browned exterior on both the bacon and the sausage.

content.text: The technique is simple: wrap, space, roast. The most common mistake is crowding the baking tray so the sausages steam rather than roast. Each piece needs a small gap around it. The bacon should be wrapped tightly so it does not unravel during cooking.
Heat the oven to 200 degrees C fan. Stretch each bacon rasher slightly by running a knife along it, which prevents it from shrinking too tightly during cooking.
Wrap one rasher of bacon tightly around each chipolata at an angle, from one end to the other. Lay seam-side down on a baking tray with a small gap between each one.
Roast for 20 to 25 minutes until the bacon fat has
and is crisp and the sausages are deeply browned. Turn once at the halfway point.

items.symptom: The bacon has unravelled during cooking
items.cause: The bacon was not wrapped tightly enough, or was placed with the seam facing up.
items.fix: Always lay seam-side down. The weight of the sausage holds the bacon in place. Stretching the bacon before wrapping also helps it grip.

items.symptom: The bacon is still pale and soft after 25 minutes
items.cause: The tray was overcrowded and the sausages were steaming.
items.fix: Give each one at least 2 cm of space on the tray. Use two trays if needed. Crowded pigs in blankets never crisp.

items.cause: The heat was too high or the sausages were pricked.
items.fix: Do not prick chipolatas. The tight bacon wrap usually prevents bursting. If using thick sausages, reduce the temperature slightly.

items.symptom: The bacon is crisp but the sausage inside is still pink
items.cause: The bacon coloured faster than the sausage cooked through.
items.fix: Use chipolatas rather than thicker sausages: they cook through in the same time as the bacon crisps. A thermometer in the thickest sausage should read 75 degrees C.

content.text: Brush with a teaspoon of honey and a pinch of chilli flakes before roasting for a sweet-spiced version. Use smoked bacon for a stronger flavour. For a larger batch with more depth, use Cumberland sausages instead of chipolatas and wrap in two rashers each.

Pigs in blankets are the most universally loved element of the British Christmas dinner, rated higher than the turkey itself in most surveys. The name is also used in the United States for a different dish entirely. The British version of bacon-wrapped sausages became a Christmas table fixture through the second half of the twentieth century and is now mandatory.
